title: Copper LME Stocks Plunge to Two-Year Low as Physical Demand Surges
description: Copper LME Stocks Plunge to Two-Year Low as Physical Demand Surges Kitco News
htmlContent:
Copper LME Stocks Plunge to Two-Year Low as Physical Demand Surges

By Neils Christensen, Kitco News • June 6, 2026

(Kitco News) – Copper inventories held in London Metal Exchange warehouses fell to their lowest level in over two years on Friday, dropping to 79,300 tonnes — a figure that represents barely 18 hours of global consumption and is widely seen by traders as a critical signal of physical market tightness.

The drawdown of approximately 7,900 tonnes in a single week was attributed by market participants to strong offtake from copper fabricators in China, Europe and the United States, particularly from manufacturers supplying components to data centre construction projects and electrical transmission grid upgrades.

"We are seeing cancellations of warrants at a pace that is genuinely alarming from a supply security standpoint," said Peter Fertig, director at QCB Financial Consultants in Frankfurt. "At this rate of withdrawal, the LME buffer could be effectively exhausted within weeks unless fresh material arrives from mines."

The shortage of immediately available copper is already having commercial consequences. Spot copper premiums in Shanghai rose to $145 per tonne above the LME benchmark on Friday, compared to around $80 just six weeks ago. European premiums also moved sharply higher.

Analysts noted that the tightness comes at a particularly sensitive time. Chinese consumption typically accelerates in the second half of the year as construction and manufacturing activity peaks, while the US and European grid modernisation programmes are scaling up procurement. Meanwhile, new mine supply from the major projects expected to provide relief — including Kamoa-Kakula Phase 3 in the Democratic Republic of Congo and the Resolution copper project in Arizona — remains at least two to three years from meaningful contribution.

LME three-month copper held above $10,900 per tonne on Friday afternoon, near its highest level since 2024, while COMEX copper futures also pushed toward $5.00 per pound.

"The market is effectively telling you that there is no easy fix on the supply side," said Anna Staszak, metals analyst at Marex. "Copper's structural deficit story is playing out in real time in the LME warehouse data."

Tags: copper, LME, inventory, warehouse, physical demand, data center, grid, supply deficit

Sentiment signal: bullish
